coredata.dc:title: Controllable electrochemical/electroanalytical approach to generate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from varied amino acids: pinpointing the utmost quantum yield and the versatile photoluminescent and electrochemiluminescent applications
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Fushuang
Xu, Yuanhong
Liu, Jixian
Song, Zhongqian
Liu, Mengli
Liu, Jingquan
coredata.dc:description: Despite the great achievements been made on the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) investigations, evaluation criteria including simple synthesis unit, speediness capability, green precursor, high quantum yield (QY), stable and excellent physico-chemical properties as well as versatile applicability are still pursued by an ideal preparation method or satisfied products of CQDs. In addition, it is in urgent need but seldom reported to accurately position the utmost QY point for one synthetic cycle using a simple manipulation way. Herein, multifunctional nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized using various amino acids (AAs) as the precursors via a simple three-electrode electrochemical/electroanalytical system in less than two hours. Most importantly, the optimum reaction time for generating N-CQDs with utmost QY can be straightforwardly and accurately derived via the maximum current value on the amperometric i-t curve recorded during the EC process. The N-CQDs can be well dispersed in aqueous medium with narrow size distribution and average size of 2.95±0.12nm as evidenced by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Both the distinct and stable photoluminescent and electrochemiluminescent properties were observed for the as-prepared N-CQDs. The N-CQDs obtained from different AAs have similar optical and ECL features under the same EC conditions, while aspartic acid based N-CQDs exhibited the highest QY of 46.2%. These N-CQDs can be extensively applied in efficient cell imaging, fiber staining and specific sensitive detection towards ferric ion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: electrochemical/electroanalytical
pinpointing utmost quantum yield
fluorescent imaging
label-free specifc ion detection
electrochemiluminescent

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of the Asp-N-CQDs in specific Fe3+ sensing
Application of the Asp-N-CQDs in fluorescent staining
Application of the Asp-N-CQDs in cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the setup and the proposed generation mechanism for N-CQDs preparation from AAs under alkaline condition via the bottom-up ECA strategy using a three-electrode system containing two Pt sheets and Ag/AgCl as the working, auxiliary electrodes reference electrode, respectively. Accordingly, the as-prepared N-CQDs suspensions showed bright blue luminescence under UV light illumination at 365nm.

(a) The amperometric i-t curve at applied potential of 3V and (b) corresponding QYs evaluated at different reaction time for the N-CQDs generation using a three-electrode EAC system. Photographs surrounding the curves are collected for the reaction mixtures at different time periods during the EAC generation process, respectively. Electrolyte component: 0.5M Asp solution in the presence of 3M NH3 H2O.

(A-C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of different N-CQDs and the corresponding photographs of the as-prepared N-CQDs suspension under room light (inset, left) and upon irradiation with 365nm UV light (inset, right): (D-F) optimal excitation (ex) and emission (em) PL spectra and (G-I) emission spectra of the as-prepared N-CQDs at progressively increasing excitation wavelengths from 270nm to 450nm: (A, D, G) Cys-N-CQDs, (B, E, H) Asp-N-CQDs, (C, F, I) Arg-N-CQDs.
The correlation between QYs of N-CQDs and (A) the concentration of NH3 H2O or (B) the applied voltage.

(A) Representative TEM image and (B) the particle size distribution obtained from TEM, (C) HRTEM image, (D) amplified HRTEM image, (E) the AFM image and (F) the corresponding height profile along the line in (E) of the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs suspension.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs: (A) survey XPS data; narrow scan spectra of (B) C1s, (C) O1s and(D) N1s, respectively.
FTIR Spectra of pure Asp and the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs.

Effects of (A) background pH, (B) ionic strength and (C) long-term UV irradiation on the PL intensity of Asp-N-CQDs, respectively. (D) PL spectra of (1) fresh Asp-N-CQDs, upon (2) one and (3) two months storage.

(A) Comparison of PL intensities for Asp-N-CQDs upon addition of different metal ions at the same concentrations (400μM). (B) Schematic illustration of the mechanism for PL quenching of CQDs by Fe3+. (C) PL emission spectra of the Asp-N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions at different concentrations. (D) The correlation between the Fe3+ concentration and PL intensity at 350nm. Error bars indicate standard deviations of the means (n=3).

(A) Bright-field and (B, C) fluorescent images of cotton fiber stained with 100mgmL−1 Asp-N-CQDs. (D) Bright-field and (E, F) fluorescent images of silk fiber stained with 100mgmL−1 Asp-N-CQDs. The fluorescent images were obtained at the excitation wavelengths of 450−480nm, and 510−550nm. Scale bar: 50μm.

(A) Cell viability of the HeLa cells (n=3, mean±S.D.) after being incubated with Asp-N-CQDs at various concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 and 300μgmL−1), respectively. LSCM images of the HeLa cells incubated with Asp-N-CQDs at a concentration of 100μgmL−1 for 2h at 37°C: (B) fluorescent images and (C) bright field images when the excitation wavelength was set at 488nm. The image (D) is the merged image of (B) and (C).

(A) ECL intensity-potential curves of 100mgmL−1 Asp-N-CQDs in 100mM PBS (pH 9.0) (a) with and (b) without 10mMK2S2O8, (c) 10mMK2S2O8 alone. The scan rate is 100mVs−1. (B) ECL intensity–time curve under continuous CV scanning for 1000s (25 cycles) for Asp-N-CQDs/K2S2O8 system.

head.ce:title: Controllable electrochemical/electroanalytical approach to generate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from varied amino acids: pinpointing the utmost quantum yield and the versatile photoluminescent and electrochemiluminescent applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Micro/Nano Luminescent and Electrochemical Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Institute for Graphene Applied Technology Innovation, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textiles, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Collaborative Innovation Center for Marine Biomass Fibers Materials and Textiles of Shandong Province, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Micro/Nano Luminescent and Electrochemical Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering
Institute for Graphene Applied Technology Innovation
Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textiles
The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory
Collaborative Innovation Center for Marine Biomass Fibers Materials and Textiles of Shandong Province

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Fushuang Niu and Yuanhong Xu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Controllable electrochemical/electroanalytical approach was developed to generate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) from varied amino acids. The utmost N-CQDs quantum yields (QYs, 46.2%) can be straightforwardly pinpointed via the maximum current value on the amperometric i-t curve. The N-CQDs were with stable photoluminescent and electrochemiluminescent (ECL) properties and could be extensively applied in fluorescent bioimaging and label-free specific Fe3+ detection.

Despite the great achievements been made on the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) investigations, evaluation criteria including simple synthesis unit, speediness capability, green precursor, high quantum yield (QY), stable and excellent physico-chemical properties as well as versatile applicability are still pursued by an ideal preparation method or satisfied products of CQDs. In addition, it is in urgent need but seldom reported to accurately position the utmost QY point for one synthetic cycle using a simple manipulation way. Herein, multifunctional nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized using various amino acids (AAs) as the precursors via a simple three-electrode electrochemical/electroanalytical system in less than two hours. Most importantly, the optimum reaction time for generating N-CQDs with utmost QY can be straightforwardly and accurately derived via the maximum current value on the amperometric i-t curve recorded during the EC process. The N-CQDs can be well dispersed in aqueous medium with narrow size distribution and average size of 2.95±0.12nm as evidenced by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Both the distinct and stable photoluminescent and electrochemiluminescent properties were observed for the as-prepared N-CQDs. The N-CQDs obtained from different AAs have similar optical and ECL features under the same EC conditions, while aspartic acid based N-CQDs exhibited the highest QY of 46.2%. These N-CQDs can be extensively applied in efficient cell imaging, fiber staining and specific sensitive detection towards ferric ion.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [1–3] have attracted intense interesting due to their excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties, high stability, chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity, well dispersibility in aqueous medium, high resistance to photo-bleaching, environment friendliness, low cost and good biocompatibility. All these unique properties make CQDs appealing fluorescent materials in optoelectronics [4–6], photocatalysis [7], energy conversion [8–10], bioimaging [11–14], chemical sensing [15,16], drug delivery [17], etc. Many methods have been developed for CQDs preparation including electrochemical (EC) methods [18,19], laser ablation [20,21], microwave assisted techniques [22,23] and hydrothermal treatment. Among them, EC route possesses obvious superiorities such as simple operation, mild reaction conditions, rapidness, environmental friendliness, cost effectiveness, etc. Especially, the three-electrode electrochemical/electroanalytical (ECA) modes such as cyclic voltammetry, potentiostatic method can not only exactly control the applied potential for CQDs generation, but also automatically give visible statistical data such as current-time curves for further analysis [24–26].

EC methods for CQDs generation can be achieved through two ways: “top-down” or “bottom-up”. The top-down strategies are mostly studied ones, which involve exfoliation of bulky carbon-rich precursors such as graphite, carbon paste, carbon black, graphene oxide, carbon nanotube, or carbon fiber electrodes [18,23,27–29] into fine particles less than 10nm in diameter. These methods have advantages of abundant raw materials as well as mass production but often suffer from disadvantages such as uncontrollable morphology, non-uniformly distributed sizes and low quantum yield of the CQDs [30–32].The bottom-up EC ones, which are utilized to synthesize CQDs through electro-oxidation, electro-polymerization, carbonization and passivation from small molecular precursors such as ethanol [33,34] and nitriles [35], offer advantages of precisely controlling over the morphology and the size distribution as well as convenience for surface-passivation or heteroatom doping of the CQDs. However, CQDs synthesized via the bottom-up EC methods are less reported than expected, since special and green precursors have to be appropriately selected to make sure the as-prepared CQDs are non-toxic and bio-compatible for extensive bioapplications.

It was suggested that precursors possessing amino or carboxyl groups are favorable for the preparation of CQDs with high water-dispersibility [35,36]. As the basic building units of protein, amino acids (AAs) have advantages of excellent biocompatibility and abundant both amino and carboxyl groups, which also enable them to be easily cross-linked through dehydration and condensation polymerization reactions between different AAs [36]. Furthermore, the rich content of heteroatoms especially nitrogen in AAs is supposed to be able to dope the heteroatoms into the carbon framework of the as-prepared CQDs and affect their PL properties accordingly. These advantages thus made AAs ideal green raw materials for the bottom-up hydrothermal or microwave preparation of CQDs with multifunctional groups [34,36,37].Nevertheless, it is still a great challenge to study how to choose the optimum type of AAs as precursor for the generation of high-quality CQDs via bottom-up EC methods.

Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY), the ratio of fluorescence intensity to absorbed light, is a significant criteria to assess an excellent fluorescence reagent that is with strong brightness by low excitation intensity [38]. To identify the utmost QY of a synthesized CQDs, it has to be either roughly estimated through visual inspection of the solution color change [39] or accurately figured out via complex procedures and massive data calculation to optimize the reaction parameters [40], thus it is in urgent need to accurately position the utmost QY point for one synthetic cycle. In addition, besides the most frequently studied PL properties for the CQDs, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is also an interesting characteristic to evaluate the significative functions and broaden the versatile applicability of the CQDs [41], while the ECL features of the CQDs generated by the bottom-up EC methods were seldom reported.

Herein, a simple and efficient bottom-up EC strategy was proposed for the generation of multifunctional nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using AAs as the green precursors. Based on the three-electrode ECA system, not only water-dispersible N-CQDs with strong PL and stable ECL properties were obtained, one simple and direct-vision way was also developed for identifying the utmost QY point during one synthetic cycle of CQDs generation. Moreover, the N-CQDs from different tested AAs have similar optical and ECL features, while aspartic acid (Asp) based N-CQDs (Asp-N-CQDs) were optimized to have the highest QY among the studied AAs-based ones under the same EC conditions. The N-CQDs showed uniformly spherical morphology, narrow size distribution as evidenced by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Other techniques including atomic force microscopy (AFM), UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy were applied for the characterization of the N-CQDs and studying the possible generation mechanism. The as-optimized N-CQDs exhibited highly selective quenching response to Fe3+ ion, which can be used to establish a specific Fe3+ sensor. Besides, the good PL and low cytotoxicity enables the CQDs to be promising fluorescent materials in cell imaging and fiber staining.

L-cysteine (Cys), L-aspartic acid (Asp), L-serine (Ser), L-methionine (Met), L-histidine (His) and L-arginine (Arg) (AR), the dialysis bag (MD34, cut off molecular weight 3500Da) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye. 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, AR), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR), potassium hydroxide (KOH, AR), sodium chloride (NaCl, AR), potassium chloride (KCl, AR), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4, AR), mono potassium phosphate (KH2PO4, AR) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared containing NaCl (8g), KCl (0.2g), Na2HPO4 (1.44g), and KH2PO4 (0.24g) in 1.0L solution. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm−1) was obtained with a Milli-Q ultrapure system (Qingdao, China).

All EC measurements were performed with a conventional three-electrode cell with a CHI 760D electrochemical work station (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). The FL and UV absorption spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) and a Mapada UV-1800PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China), respectively. The morphology of N-CQDs was observed on a JEOL Ltd JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by using a SPI3800N microscope (Seiko Instruments Inc.) operating in the tapping mode. AFM samples were prepared by depositing the N-CQDs dispersions on freshly cleaved mica surfaces through a drop-casting method, respectively, and then were left to air-dry. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Scientific Instruments Corp., USA). XPS data were obtained on an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer (VG Scientific, West Sussex, U. K.) using 300W Al Kα radiation. The fluorescent images of the cells were taken using the LEICA TCS SP2 laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, Leica Microsystems Heidelberg GmbH, Germany) with a 100×oil immersion objective. The N-CQDs cultured cells were excited using 488nm Ar laser.

The QYs of the Asp-N-CQDs were determined on the basis of previously established procedure. Typically, quinine sulfate (literature quantum yield 0.54) in 0.1M H2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the reference [42,43]. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, the aqueous suspension of N-CQDs and the aqueous solution of reference sample were diluted to keep the absorption intensity below 0.1 and 0.05, respectively, at the excitation wavelength of 350nm. The quantum yield of the N-CQDs was calculated using the equation below [44]:
Φ
X
=
Φ
ST
(
Grad X
Grad
ST
)
(
η
X 2
η
ST
2
)
where the subscripts ST and X refer to quinine sulfate and N-CQDs respectively, Φ represents the PL QY. Grad represents the gradient from the plot of integrated PL intensity vs. absorbance, and η the refractive index of the solvent.

The freeze-dried N-CQDs was dispersed in PBS solution (pH=6) to reach a concentration of 1.0mgmL−1 Asp-N-CQDs. Different kinds of metal ion storage solutions (Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+) all at concentrations of 0.12M were prepared, respectively. To evaluate the selectivity of Asp-N-CQDs towards the metal ions, 10μL metal ion storage solution was mixed with the 2990μL Asp-N-CQDs suspension, and then the PL spectra were measured at the maximum excitation wavelength of 360nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of Asp-N-CQDs upon the addition of different final concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 1000μM were investigated.

The bioimaging test for the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs was performed using HeLa cells. The cells (106 cells per sample) were seeded onto 35mm glass chamber slides, followed by incubation in culture medium, Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM), containing the Asp-N-CQDs at a concentration of 100μgmL−1 for 24h. To remove the free and physically absorbed Asp-N-CQDs, all cells were washed with PBS buffer three times at room temperature. Finally, the cellular images were obtained using a LEICA TCS SP2 laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, Leica Microsystems Heidelberg GmbH, Germany) with a 488nm Ar laser.

The cytotoxicity of the Asp-N-CQDs was evaluated using a standard MTT assay. The cells were incubated with different concentrations of Asp-N-CQDs (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300μgmL−1, respectively) in fresh DMEM for 24h. The cultured cells were added to DMEM containing MTT (10mL, 5mgmL−1) and further incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for another 4h. The MTT containing medium was then added with 100μL DMSO to dissolve the formazan crystal precipitate. The cells incubated with PBS in the absence of Asp-N-CQDs were taken as the control (viability defined as 100%), and six parallel samples incubated with Asp-N-CQDs were performed under the same conditions for each independent experiment. Finally, the absorption at 490nm of each well was measured for calculation of the cell survival rates using an EL808 ultramicroplate reader (Bio-TEK Instrument, Inc., Winooski, VT, USA) [45].

The ECL behaviors of N-CQDs in 100mM PBS at different pH values (pH=6∼10) containing 20mMK2S2O8 were studied. A three-electrode system containing two Pt sheets and Ag/AgCl (saturated with KCl) as working, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively, was used. CV experiments scanning from −2.0 to 0V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 were performed with a CH Instruments 760D voltammetric analyzer. ECL intensities were collected by a MPI-E multifunctional chemiluminescent analytical system (Xi'an Remex Electronics Co. Ltd., China) with a photomultiplier tube voltage set at 800V.

The N-CQDs were prepared via a three-electrode EC system using two Pt sheets (1.0cm2) and Ag/AgCl as the working, auxiliary and reference electrodes, respectively. AAs aqueous solution containing 3M NH3 H2O was used as the electrolyte. A potentiostatic mode was applied for the EC generation of the N-CQDs, and amperometric i-t curve was collected to study the current variation simultaneously. As can be seen in Fig. 1 , when a constant potential of 3V was applied, the color of the electrolyte solution changed from colorless (0s) to yellow (300s) and then to orange (1000s) and brown (2000s), respectively. With the ongoing process, dark brown suspension was observed at 4000s. During the period from 4000 to 9000s, the color of the suspension cannot be differentiated obviously by naked eyes. Additionally, it can be observed that the electrolyte solution became shallow gradually (e.g. 10800, 14000 and 34200s) accompanying with increasing sediments formed near the bottom of the reaction vessel. Subsequently, the solution changed to colorless again and large amount of precipitates were formed at the time of 68400s. To more clearly observe the obtained resultant precipitates, amplified photographs for the reaction mixtures at different reaction time from 14400 to 68400s during the EAC generation process was further collected in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information.

Different N-CQD suspensions at various time point were collected and the QYs were derived accordingly (curve a in Fig. 1). Curve b of Fig. 1 represents the amperometric i-t curve recorded during the CQDs generation. It was observed that both the QYs and current increased with the darkening color of the electrolyte solution and decreased with the color degrading. More interestingly, it was discovered that the utmost point of the QYs of the product suspension fitted well with the maximum current value on the amperometric i-t curve (see Fig. 1). To verify whether this interesting phenomenon is a special case or universal one, both QYs and amperometric i-t curves were recorded for various other AAs solutions under various applied EC potentials, respectively. Similar phenomena were obtained for all the studied AAs. As a result, it is supposed that the ECA methods can not only be used for CQDs generation but also for accurately positioning the utmost QY point for one synthetic cycle. For example, although the utmost QY was achieved at 6315s, after which the QYs of the N-CQDs began to decrease gradually due to the formation of the sediments, the color change of the electrolyte solution as well as small amount of sediments at the vicinity of maximum QYs (4000–9000s) were difficult to be precisely distinguished by the naked eyes, thus the optimal reaction time for CQDs generation could not be identified accordingly. While the proposed amperometric i-t mode of the ECA method provides a simple, accurate and direct-vision way for identifying the utmost QYs and optimizing the reaction time for the CQDs generation. Based on this EC strategy, high-quality CQDs can be obtained at minimized time.

Scheme 1 gives possible mechanism for EC formation of the N-CQDs in this experiment, which is usually via four steps including electro-oxidation, electro-polymerization, carbonization and passivation [33–35]. Generally, the electrolytic oxidation of the AAs tends to occur through an irreversible reaction (1) under alkaline conditions:
(1) RCH(NH2)COO− (AAs) + OH− →RCHO+NH3 +CO2 +2e−
NH3 will be generated from this oxidative reaction of AAs [46]. However, this reaction can be hindered upon addition of NH3 with increasing concentrations based on Le Chatelier’s principle [33,34], which indicated the necessity of NH3 H2O used as the electrolyte herein. When a constant potential of 3V was applied, the electro-oxidation of AAs was occurred to form immonium ions [RCH(NH2)+] in the presence of NH3 H2O. The RCH(NH2)+ was then reacted with the un-oxidized AAs molecules through the amidation reaction to produce RCH(NH2)CONHCHR− ions, which further underwent electro-polymerization, carbonization, and passivation to form the N-CQDs finally [33–35]. The equations (Scheme S1 in the Supporting Information) outline the possible reaction process of electro-oxidation and electro-polymerization of N-CQDs. The carbonization (e.g. formed graphite-like sp2-hybridized carbon atoms) and passivation (e.g. possessing carboxylate and ammonium functional groups) processes can be further verified through the following characterizations, including HRTEM, XPS and FTIR [33,34]. In addition, the well- dispersibility of N-CQDs in aqueous medium was indicative of the good passivation of the N-CQDs with oxygen-containing groups, which exhibited bright blue luminescence under UV light illumination at 365nm. Furthermore, with the prolonging reaction time, the electro-polymerization degree of AAs increased, resulting in enlarged size of the final products (carbon-based materials) accordingly. Too large size (e.g. larger than 100nm) of the carbon-based materials will lead to the disappearance of their quantum effect, and will not favor for their PL properties finally [14]. This was confirmed by the formation of the non-fluorescent sediments in the reaction mixture with the increasing reaction time as shown in Fig. 1 as well as Fig. S1.

Various AAs such as Cys, Asp, Ser, Met, His and Arg have been selected as precursor for CQDs generation via the proposed ECA method. It was observed that all the tested AAs can be applied to produce N-CQDs suspensions with similar optical features. Fig. 2 A–C illustrates the typical UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs generated from Cys, Asp and Arg at the same concentration, respectively. All the tested CQDs suspensions prepared by dilution of 50μL original as-prepared N-CQDs into same volume of 3mL all exhibited faint yellow under daylight with excellent aqueous dispersibility and without any obvious UV maximum absorption peakThe Cys-N-CQDs and Asp-N-CQDs suspensions showed bright blue luminescence under UV light illumination at 365nm (inset of Fig. 2A and B), while Arg-N-CQDs emitted muted blue one (inset of Fig. 2C). The PL spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths from 270 to 450nm were measured to explore their fluorescent properties. Fig. 2(D-F) shows the optimal excitation (∼360nm) and emission (∼430nm) PL spectra for Cys-N-CQDs, Asp-N-CQDs, Arg-N-CQDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(G–I), both the PL intensities and emission peaks of the as-prepared N-CQDs showed excitation-dependent PL behaviors, which were consistent with those of many previously reported fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials [33,35,37] and might be ascribed to the optical selection of differently sized N-CQDs and their surface defects [47]. As can be seen from the comparison with the previously reported CQDs obtained by other routes [48–51], the as-prepared N-CQDs herein had narrower PL emission peak with full-width half-maximum (FWHM) of 80nm, mainly resulting from a narrower size distribution (2.1-3.9nm) [52,53]. Assisted by the as-proposed ECA methods for identifying the maximum QYs for CQDs, the QYs of the Asp-N-CQDs, Cys-N-CQDs, Ser-N-CQDs, Met-N-CQDs, His-N-CQDs, Arg-N-CQDs were measured to be 46.2%, 23.6%, 18.5%, 21.3%, 15.3%, 7%, respectively, using quinine bisulfate as the reference. Based on both the optical properties and QYs, Asp was chosen as the optimized precursor for the following CQDs generation and characterizations. Meanwhile, the QYs of 46.2% for Asp-N-CQDs is much higher than those for CQDs prepared by previous EC methods [33,34].

The electrolyte component and corresponding concentrations, applied voltage and reaction time were further investigated to obtain the optimum synthetic conditions (Fig. 3 ). Firstly, as control experiments, N-CQDs cannot be geneated from Aps or NH3 H2O alone. The N-CQDs were also hardly obtained by EC treatment of Asp solution in the presence of acid or other salts such as H2SO4, HCl, NaCl or Na2SO4. When electrolyte containing NaOH, Na2HPO4 or KOH, was applied, N-CQDs can be obtained but with low QYs (<10%), which are much lower than that obtained in the presence of NH3 H2O. All these confirmed that NH3 H2O played a crucial role in preparation of N-CQDs in this work. The effects of NH3 H2O concentration on the QYs of the as-prepared N-CQDs were also investigated.As shown in Fig. 3A, 3M was found to be the optimal NH3 H2O concentration for the generation of N-CQDs with the best QYs. Upon further increasing the concentration of NH3 H2O, the reaction between carboxylate and ammonium groups was hindered, and thus the formation of N-CQDs decreased [34].

The influence of different applied potentials (1.0–7.0V) on the preparation of CQDs was investigated using 0.5M Asp containing 3M NH3 H2O as electrolyte. It was found that when 0.5V was applied, the color of electrolyte still remained colorless even after 2hours and N-CQDs with QYs of less than 2.1% were reached, which is probably due to the lower oxidation current. However, when the potential of 7V was applied, uneven and relatively larger sized N-CQDs with relatively low QYs (25.3%) were observed owning to much higher current applied (Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information). After optimization it was found that 3V was the most suitable potential for the preparation of Asp-N-CQDs.

Fig. 4 A displays the typical TEM image of Asp-N-CQDs prepared under the optimized ECA conditions, which showed that the Asp-N-CQDs can be well monodispersed in aqueous medium with uniform spherical morphology. The diameter of the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs was found to be 2.95±0.12nm and a narrow size distribution was observed in Fig. 4B, which was similar to the pervious reported N-CQDs [54]. The crystalline nature of the Asp-N-CQDs was investigated by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Fig. 4C and D). An average in-plane lattice spacing of 0.210nm was observed in the crystalline structures of the Asp-N-CQDs, which agrees well with the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon and is close to the (103) diffraction plane of diamond-like (sp3) carbon [28]. The AFM image in Fig. 4E with associated height analysis revealed that the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs were homogeneous with height ranging from 1-2.5nm (Fig. 4F).

XPS analyses were carried out to investigate the composition and functional groups on the Asp-N-CQDs as well as to verify the suggested generation mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5 , there are three significant peaks at 284.8eV, 400.0eV and 532.6eV in the survey spectra corresponding to elemental species C, N and O, respectively (Fig. 5A). The strong O1s peak indicated that the oxidation reaction took place during the synthesis of Asp-N-CQDs. The C1s spectrum of the Asp-N-CQDs revealed the presence of four types of carbon bonds: C-C/CC (284.6eV), C-N (285.0eV), C-O (285.8eV) and CO (288.5eV), respectively (Fig. 4B), indicating the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs were rich in oxygenous groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups on the surfaces [55,56]. The deconvolution of the O1s spectrum verified the existence of CO (537.1eV) and C-O (533.0eV), respectively [56]. These oxygen-containing groups resulted in the excellent dispersibility of the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs in aqueous medium. The peaks at 399.8eV and 400.5eV can be assigned to C-N and N-H (Fig. 5D), respectively, which confirmed the successful doping of nitrogen element in the as-prepared CQDs [55,56].

FTIR spectroscopy was also employed to give further structural insights into the prepared Asp-N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , the conspicuous characteristic peaks at 3421.5 and 1400cm−1 should be ascribed to the stretching vibration (free) and in-plane bending vibration of the hydroxyl groups, respectively. The peak at 1618cm−1 was attributed to the CC and CN stretching in the conjugated structure. The characteristic peak at 1650cm−1 (CO) indicated the existence of carboxyl groups on the Asp-N-CQDs. The peak at 1100.8cm−1 should be due to the stretching vibrations of the C-O-C bond. All these peaks of the Asp-N-CQDs can be traced back to the FTIR spectrum of the pure Asp (Fig. 6), which ascertained the participation of Asp in the N-CQDs generation. The FTIR results are well consistent with the XPS data and again confirmed the abundant oxygenous functional groups on the surface of as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs.

The effects of pH and ionic strength of the background buffer on the PL intensity of the Asp-N-CQDs was investigated in consideration of the fact that environments with various pH and ionic strength are often encountered in practical applications. It was found that the PL intensity of the Asp-N-CQDs was slightly influenced by pH (Fig. 7 A). The zeta potential of the Asp-N-CQDs suspension was measured to be −7.8mV, indicating that the electrostatic repulsion among Asp-N-CQDs played an important role for their stability in aqueous dispersion. In addition, it was also found that the PL intensity did not significantly decreased under continuous UV irradiation and even∼80% original intensity could be maintained within 170min (Fig. 7C), reflecting the good photostability of Asp-N-CQDs. Moreover, the suspension of the Asp-N-CQDs showed long-term stability even after two-month storage in air at room temperature as evidenced by almost the unchanged PL intensity compared with the original one (Fig. 7D). All these results indicated that the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs possessed extremely high stability in various environments, which offered superiority for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the Asp-N-CQDs in specific Fe3+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the unique properties including stable photophysical properties, chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity, high resistance to photo-bleaching, environment friendly, excellent biocompatibility and so on, CQDs have been applied as excellent fluorescent probes in metal ion sensing, chemical staining and bioimaging [57–60]. The influences of different metal ions (all at 400μM), including Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ on the PL intensity of the Asp-N-CQDs in acidic conditions (pH=6.0) were studied, respectively. The Asp-N-CQDs PL intensities were significantly quenched upon the addition of Fe3+, while insignificantly or even negligibly influenced by other metal ions (Fig. 8 A). These phenomena were indicative of the specific response to Fe3+ towards the PL of the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs. As schemed in Fig. 8B, the PL quenching should be attributed to the strong interactions between Fe3+ ions and the functional oxygen-containing groups on the Asp-N-CQDs surfaces, which resulted in the photoelectron transfer from Asp-N-CQDs to Fe3+ ions [61,62].

Fe3+ ions play key roles in oxygen transport, oxygen metabolism, electronic transfer and many catalytic processes [10,61]. Since excess Fe3+ ions are harmful to health, their detection in biological systems is of great importance for human health. Therefore, the specific interactions between the Fe3+ and the Asp-N-CQDs can be used for selective measurement of Fe3+ [40]. As shown in Fig. 8C, the ratios of I0/I, where I0 and I represent the PL intensities of Asp-N-CQDs at 360nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively, indicate that 73% fluorescence of Asp-N-CQDs was quenched by the addition of 400μM Fe3+. Whereas, all other tested metal at the same concentration as Fe3+ only have slight influence on the Asp-N-CQDs PL intensity [62,63]. The PL spectra of the Asp-N-CQDs suspension at different concentrations of Fe3+ were measured. As presented in Fig. 8C the fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ was dosage dependent, therefore, can be utilized for Fe3+ quantification. The ratios of I0/I have a good linear correlation with the concentration of added Fe3+ (linear regression equation is I0/I=0.961+0.012C (R2 =0.998)) in the range of 2–600μM (Fig. 8D), which is much broader than those of recently reported values [62,64,65]. The limit of detection for Fe3+ can reach as low as 0.5μM (S/N≥3), which is much lower or comparable to those of previously reported values [40,63]. The results mentioned above clearly revealed that the Asp-N-CQDs are promising materials for Fe3+ detection in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the Asp-N-CQDs in fluorescent staining
Application of the Asp-N-CQDs in cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Low cytotoxicity is the prerequisite for the application as a good fluorescent bioimaging material. Thus, the cytotoxicity of the Asp-N-CQDs was evaluated via a standard MTT cell proliferation assay. Fig. 10 A shows the survival rates of the HeLa cells after incubation with Asp-N-CQDs for 24h at the concentrations of 0, 50, 100, 150, 150, 200, 250, 300μgmL−1, respectively. Over a period of 24h incubation, The Asp-N-CQDs at a concentration of 100μgmL−1 displayed nearly no cytotoxicity toward the HeLa cells. Beyond this concentration, the cell viability slightly decreased in a concentration-dependent behavior. The cell survival rate greater than 85% was still achieved in the culture medium with 300μgmL−1 Asp-N-CQDs. These results confirmed the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs have good biocompatibility with the cells and can be used for further cell imaging. Subsequently, HeLa cells were incubated with the Asp-N-CQDs at a concentration of 100μgmL−1 for 2h at 37 °C, followed by image acquisition via LSCM under 488nm light excitation. As shown in Fig. 10B, the HeLa cells treated with Asp-N-CQDs revealed good and reproducible morphology with bright green fluorescence under a laser illumination at 488nm. These results demonstrated the easy cellular uptake of the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs due to their excellent cyto-compatibility, which is favorable for efficient bioimaging.

ECL is one way to convert electrical energy into radiative energy, by which species emitting measurable luminescent signals were generated at electrodes via an applied voltage through high-energy electron-transfer reactions [66]. The CQDs are suggested to be a promising alternative as ECL luminophores to traditional ones because of their excellent chemical inertness, easy labeling and low toxicity. The mechanism of ECL is different from PL and not possessed by all types of CQDs. Thus ECL is also one way to broaden the applicability of CQDs. Accordingly, the ECL behavior of the optimal Asp-N-CQDs was investigated in 0.1M PBS (pH 9.0).As shown in Fig. 11 A, when the potential was cycled between −2.0 and 0V, weak ECL emission was observed. However after addition of 10mMK2S2O8, the common oxidative coreactant (or a hole donor) used in ECL systems, the Asp-N-GQDs showed an intense ECL emission at 1.92V, with an onset potential at about −0.62V (curve a). Meanwhile, no obvious ECL was observed for pure K2S2O8 without the Asp-N-CQDs (curve c). All these results indicated the important roles of Asp-N-CQDs as ECL luminophores and K2S2O8 as coreactant in the presented ECL systems. Since the pH of the buffer is a crucial factor influencing the ECL reaction of CQDs, the effect of the buffer pH on the ECL behaviors of Asp-N-CQDs was also investigated. The experimental results showed that the ECL intensity increased with the increasing pH of the buffer from 6.0 to 9.0, and tended to decrease with further increasing of pH beyond 9.0 (shown in Fig. S3 in Supporting Information). Therefore, pH 9.0 was chosen as the optimum pH value of the background buffer for this ECL system. Fig. 11B shows that the ECL emission is stable under continuous cyclic voltammetry (CV) for 25 cycles when the potential window was set between −2.0 and 0V. The relative standard deviation of the ECL peak height was 1.94%, which showed the good reproducibility and stability of the Asp-N-CQDs/K2S2O8 ECL system. The proposed ECL system was further investigated by monitoring its ECL response with intermittent usage every 6 days. As can be seen in Fig. S4, about 85% of its initial ECL intensity for Asp-N-CQDs/K2S2O8 system can be maintained after an intermitted use over the 30-day storage time, which confirmed the excellent long-term stability of the ECL system. All these results indicated that the Asp-N-CQDs possess stable and intense ECL properties and could be envisioned for broader potential applications.

In summary, multifunctional N-CQDs were prepared by a three-electrode controlled moderate ECA approach using various AAs as the precursors in less than two hours. More importantly, the optimum reaction time for generating N-CQDs with utmost QY can be straightforwardly and accurately derived via the maximum current value on the amperometric i-t curve recorded during the EC process. The N-CQDs can be well dispersed in aqueous medium with uniform morphology and average size of 2.95±0.12nm as evidenced by TEM. Both distinct and long-term stable PL and ECL properties were observed for the as-prepared N-CQDs. The N-CQDs obtained from different AAs have similar optical and ECL features under the same EC conditions, while Asp based N-CQDs exhibited the highest QY of 46.2%. The excellent PL, high stability, and low cytotoxicity of the as-prepared Asp-N-CQDs ensured their successful applications in efficient cell imaging, fiber staining and sensitive detection of Fe3+. The Asp-N-CQDs as nanosensor probes for Fe3+ detection showed wide linear range from 2 to 600μM with the detection limit of 0.5μM. The proposed method herein can meet all the criteria for evaluation of an ideal preparation method or satisfied products of CQDs, including simple synthesis unit, speediness capability, straightforward way to identify the utmost QY, green precursor, high quantum yield, stable and excellent physico-chemical properties, versatile applicability. It can be envisioned that these N-CQDs should have great potential in bio- and chem-applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21305133 and 21575071), the Qingdao Innovation Leading Expert Program, the Qingdao Basic & Applied Research Project (15-9-1-100-jch), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201601).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.03.085.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
